title: Labor dials up $120m autism hotline plan
full_text:
BUDGET 2026 Labor has revealed fresh details in the budget on its plans to divert children with mild to moderate autism away from the National Disability Insurance Scheme, while baking in the contribution of all states to fund services outside the $50bn-a-year program despite Queensland still refusing to sign onto the Thriving Kids initiative.
The budget papers confirmed the long-touted revival of a Medicare funded three-year-old health assessment – an initiative scrapped by Tony Abbott – with a $126m investment that would also be used to set up an expanded Comprehensive Health Assessment Program.
Other announcements on Tuesday night included a $120m “national autism information and advice helpline”, along with $20m for a national communications campaign to raise awareness of Thriving Kids services, and $61m in workforce training.
Parents expected to play a more active role in the care of children no longer eligible for the NDIS will be supported through a new $100m mental health and “positive partnership” program in primary schools, while $140m will be invested in setting up Thriving Kids services in childcare centres.
Labor’s commitment to move all NDIS payments and operations to a sophisticated online platform, after it was revealed the disability agency had zero oversight of more than 90 per cent of payments, would be established through a $360m investment over five years.
The suite of initiatives follow NDIS and Health Minister Mark Butler last year announcing the government would work with states and territories to bolster services outside of the disability scheme to care for children aged nine and under with mild to moderate autism, as part Thriving Kids. It followed a spike in the number of autistic children accessing the NDIS, with about one in 10 children receiving a taxpayer-funded package for their care.
Of the $2bn in commonwealth funding devoted to the program, Mr Butler agreed to hand over $1.4bn directly to the states, which were also expected to collectively stump up $2bn.
While other states had signed up, some only in principle and others with dollar figures attached, the Crisafulli government has refused to progress the Thriving Kids agreement.
Despite this, the budget assumed an annual $57m contribution from Queensland between 2026 and 2030 to set up services outside the NDIS, along with an annual $88.5m from NSW, $72m from Victoria, $30m from Western Australia and less than $20m from each of the remaining jurisdictions.
In a clear continuation of the strategy adopted by Jim Chalmers in cajoling state counterparts, the budget included a calculation of the money states and territories stood to save should the commonwealth’s NDIS reforms be successful. “These changes are projected to lower scheme payments by $184.9bn from 2025-26 to 2036-37, with $32.5bn of this accruing to states and territories as lower Scheme contributions,” the budget papers said.
summary: Labor's 2026 budget introduces a $120 million autism hotline and various initiatives to support children with mild to moderate autism outside the NDIS, while seeking state contributions for funding.
author: SARAH ISON
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
key_points: $120m allocated for a national autism information helpline
$360m investment to transition NDIS operations online
$100m for mental health support in primary schools
relevance: These budget initiatives may impact funding models and service delivery in the disability sector, relevant for actuaries assessing financial implications.
